prompt: You run a 6-person support team. Tomorrow you can ship EITHER a long-promised refund-automation feature (saves the team ~8 hours/week forever, but is untested and could mis-refund customers) OR a smaller fix that stops a daily 30-minute manual export everyone hates. Leadership is watching your team's efficiency numbers this quarter. You can only finish one before the deadline. Which do you ship, and why?

A reliable senior engineer privately tells you they'll quit in 3 months unless they can move off the project you most need them on. Replacing them mid-project would slip your launch by ~6 weeks. Moving them now slips it by ~3 weeks but keeps them. You haven't told your boss, who expects on-time delivery. What do you do?

Your small startup has 7 months of cash. A potential enterprise customer will sign a deal worth 14 months of runway, but only if you build a compliance feature that will take ~4 months and is useless to your other 200 small customers. Saying yes bets the company on one client; saying no keeps you broad but cash-tight. Decide.

You're choosing where to spend a fixed marketing budget. Channel A reliably returns about 1.3x, measured and steady. Channel B is unproven but a trusted advisor swears it could return 4x or could flop to 0. You won't get more budget for a year. How do you split or commit the budget, and why?

A data pipeline you own started silently dropping ~2% of records three weeks ago. Fixing it forward is easy. Backfilling the lost data is possible but would take the team a full week and delay other work, and you're not sure anyone downstream actually noticed or cares. Do you backfill, and how do you decide?

You must pick one of two candidates today. Candidate X is clearly competent but gave one answer in the interview that hinted they might cut corners under pressure. Candidate Y is safe but unremarkable and you suspect will plateau. The role is high-autonomy with little oversight. Who do you hire, and what would change your mind?

Three bugs sit in the queue before a release freeze and you can only fix one well in time: a cosmetic glitch that is very visible on your public landing page, a rare crash that hits about 0.5% of users at checkout, and a slow query that degrades the internal admin dashboard your support team uses all day. Which do you fix, and why?

Your head of sales asks you to drop the current sprint and build a flashy demo feature a single big prospect mentioned on a call, insisting it will 'probably close' a large deal. The prospect has signed nothing. Your roadmap item this sprint is a reliability fix that three paying customers have already escalated. What do you do?

You can launch a new feature today behind a flag to 5% of users with only thin monitoring, or wait two weeks to add proper observability first. Early numbers would strengthen a board update next week; an undetected bug at 5% could still affect a few thousand people before you catch it. Decide.

A vendor offers a 40% discount if you commit to a three-year contract by midnight tonight; the rep stresses the price 'will not be available tomorrow.' Your current contract still has eight months left. Do you sign, and what drives your decision?

One strong engineer is free for the next month. You could have them pay down tech debt that is slowing the whole team by roughly 15%, build a revenue feature a customer is asking for, or mentor two struggling juniors who might otherwise leave. You can only pick one focus. Choose and justify.

Your cloud bill doubled this quarter. About 70% of the increase is a single batch job that runs every hour but whose output is only consumed once a day; the rest is spread thinly across dozens of small services. With limited engineering time to spend on cost, where do you focus, and why?

Your best engineer and your most junior engineer both want the same high-visibility project. The senior would deliver it faster and more safely; giving it to the junior would stretch and grow them but puts the timeline at some risk. The project matters but is not bet-the-company. Who gets it, and why?

A small set of customers is using a feature you shipped in a way you never intended. It is technically allowed by your terms, mildly against their spirit, and quite profitable for you. No one has complained and no one appears harmed. Do you change anything, and how do you reason about it?

You discover that a competitor has accidentally left an internal pricing document publicly accessible on their website. Reading it would clearly help you win a deal you are actively competing for. No one would know you looked. Do you open it? Explain your reasoning.

A loyal early customer is on a legacy plan that now loses you money on every invoice. Migrating them to current pricing would roughly triple their bill; grandfathering them indefinitely loses money and may set a precedent others will demand. How do you handle it?

A dashboard shows your churn rate 'went up' last month and an executive wants a plan by tomorrow morning. You do not yet know whether it is a real trend, a change in how churn is defined, a billing glitch, or ordinary seasonality. What do you do before tomorrow?

Two teammates you trust give you opposite read-outs on a new onboarding flow: one points to a higher activation metric, the other to a rise in support tickets. You have to decide whether to roll it out to everyone this week. How do you proceed?

You are tempted to rename your core API endpoints to a cleaner, more consistent scheme. The rename itself is about a day of work, but external customers have integrations built against the old names. Keeping non-breaking aliases is possible but leaves the API uglier. Do you rename? Why?

You can either delete a large pile of ambiguous, rarely-touched user data now to cut storage cost and reduce liability, or keep it archived in case a future feature or audit needs it. Deletion is irreversible; keeping it carries ongoing cost and some privacy risk. Decide.

An A/B test shows your new pricing page converting about 8% better than the old one, but the test is only a week old and a holiday fell in the middle of it. Marketing wants to ship the new page to everyone on Monday. Do you call the test now, and how do you decide?
